persona.name: Ari Lin Bennett

occupation.title: Pharmacy Technician (part-time); occasional gig worker (delivery and on-demand retail shifts)
occupation.organization: Independent community pharmacy and contract delivery platforms
occupation.description: Ari works roughly 25-30 hours/week as a certified pharmacy technician at a community pharmacy near downtown Minneapolis. Responsibilities include: filling prescriptions under pharmacist supervision, managing OTC shelf inventory (including skincare categories), counseling patients on basic product choice (especially fragrance-free/ hypoallergenic OTC options), entering insurance claims, performing point-of-sale transactions, and stocking supplies. They also spend occasional evenings and weekends taking short on-demand shifts delivering packages and groceries to supplement income. Their pharmacy role gives them familiarity with common topical active ingredients (hydrocortisone, niacinamide, ceramides, petrolatum), preservative systems, and the practical constraints of pricing and insurance. They interact frequently with local dermatology offices when clarifying prescriptions or verifying compounded topical formulations. Ari is practical, reliable on shift, and often trusted by regular customers for calm, nonjudgmental recommendations.

persona.gender: Prefers not to disclose or other gender identity, may avoid explicitly gendered marketing and choose products by ingredient lists and peer recommendations
persona.education: Associate of Applied Science in Pharmacy Technology from Hennepin Technical College. Completed additional non-credit coursework in dermatological skincare basics through a regional community college; attended weekend seminars hosted by local dermatologists on atopic dermatitis and barrier repair.
long_term_goals: To build a modest, stable life with the flexibility to work freelance when desired.
To save enough to move into a small condo in a safe, walkable Minneapolis neighborhood within five years.
To cultivate a small side business curating fragrance-free, cruelty-free skincare bundles and a reputation as a trustworthy ingredient researcher.
To be financially independent and reduce reliance on gig work, possibly through a stable, full-time role in a healthcare setting.
To travel a few times a year on a tight budget to visit museums and small cultural festivals in North American cities.
To be recognized within their online community as someone who helps others with sensitive skin through patient, evidence-focused advice.
To develop enough savings to take a part-time course in public health or dermatology assistance to increase earning potential.
To maintain physical and mental health routines that keep skin flares manageable and reduce anxiety around finances.
To one day adopt a small dog or cat and create a calm, low-stimulation home environment.
To keep learning and remain curious about other cultures, cuisines, and ingredient traditions without adopting faddish trends uncritically.
persona.style: Quiet, understated, and precise. Speaks softly with a mild Upper Midwest American cadence—words measured, often pausing to choose the least presumptive phrasing. Prefers email or text to phone calls when communicating outside immediate face-to-face interactions. Uses inclusive, non-gendered language when addressing others and avoids marketing-style superlatives. Dress is neutral, functional, and unbranded: thrifted jeans, plain tees layered with a lightweight cardigan, and a simple silver chain or small studs. Mannerisms include: reading ingredient labels aloud to self under fluorescent store lights, tapping a fingertip against the back of the hand when thinking through a decision, keeping a small pocket notebook in their backpack for product notes and prices, and using a pale-blue silicone patch tester at home to trial new items. On social media they favor long-form YouTube deep dives and quiet micro-blogging; they rarely post selfies and instead share photos of ingredient lists, flat-lay product shoots, and concise review notes. In conversation, Ari defaults to listening, asks clarifying questions, and gives practical examples rather than emotional platitudes. They avoid loud music venues and prefer small coffee shops with soft lighting and unobtrusive playlists.

traits: Introverted and reflective—recharges with solitude and small routines.
Cautious and deliberative—tests products and ideas before committing.
Highly empathetic toward people with health anxieties; patient in one-on-one support.
Detail-oriented—keeps spreadsheets of product expiry dates, costs, and patch test results.
Curious—enjoys ingredient science and cross-cultural product traditions.
Skeptical of hype—requires evidence or repeated anecdotal confirmation before trying viral items.
Value-driven—prioritizes cruelty-free claims and ethical labor practices in purchases.
Budget-conscious but willing to splurge on a trustworthy 'hero' product on sale.
Routine-loving—prefers a minimal, repeatable skin regimen rather than daily experimentation.
Anxious under financial uncertainty—practices conservative spending habits.
Helpful but private—will give thorough advice online, but is selective about personal disclosure.
Resilient—has navigated allergies and work instability without losing long-term optimism.

interests: Ingredient science and reading PubMed summaries or dermatologist Q&A transcripts.
Low-key coffee shops with good lighting for reading and writing.
Thrift shopping for neutral wardrobe staples.
Documentary-style YouTube channels about dermatology, evidence-based beauty, and manufacturing.
Minimal-step skincare routines emphasizing barrier repair.
Community forums (Reddit threads, specialized skincare FB groups) for long-form reviews.
Subscription boxes with an emphasis on clinical samples or travel-sized fragrance-free items.
Cooking simple fusion recipes inspired by mixed cultural background—stews, rice bowls, marinades.
Listening to slow podcasts about urban planning, healthcare policy, or indie music.
Visiting local pop-up shops for new indie DTC skincare brands, but only to read labels, not impulse-buy.
Budget travel to museums and local arts festivals.
Journaling product experiences after patch tests.
DIY home-care like mixing ceramide-rich moisturizers into lightweight bases.
Trying one new skincare sample per month on a patch area.
Collecting plain glass sample jars and repurposing packaging.
Following a handful of regional dermatologists and pharmacists on social media.
Comparing ingredient lists across price tiers to learn about formulation trade-offs.
Reading long-form investigative pieces about brand supply chains.
Listening to user-submitted testimonials and following verified reviewer codes of conduct.
Occasional amateur photography of ingredient lists and product textures for personal reference.
skincare_preferences: Prefers fragrance-free formulations (no added perfumes or botanical scent blends).
Seeks ceramide-rich moisturizers and products labeled 'barrier repair'.
Favors lightweight, water-based serums that layer well under sunscreen and makeup.
Likes multi-purpose items—essence-serum hybrids or a moisturizer that contains an active but not multiple actives.
Avoids sulfates in cleansers; prefers gentle surfactants like sodium cocoyl isethionate or decyl glucoside.
Avoids parabens and certain formaldehyde-releasers as preservatives (prefers phenoxyethanol or well-tolerated alternatives when tolerated).
Looks for minimal ingredient lists; fewer fillers and unnecessary botanical extracts.
Interested in niacinamide at low-to-moderate concentrations; cautious with retinoids and strong acids.
Favors products formulated with ceramides, cholesterol, fatty acids, glycerin, and hyaluronic acid for hydration.
Likes packaging that protects formula (pump or airless) rather than open jars for preservative-sensitive items.
Prefers clear labeling of pH values for at-home acids and transparent mention of active concentrations where provided.
Avoids essential oils and limonene/ linalool high formulations due to fragrance sensitivity.
Buys travel sizes and samples first and maintains a small rotation rather than many open products.
Subscribes to one sample-based subscription box and one DTC brand auto-replenishment for a trusted moisturizer.
Prefers cruelty-free certification (Leaping Bunny or company statement plus transparent audits).
Values sustainable sourcing notes but prioritizes human labor ethics and transparent supply chains.
Willing to pay $30–$70 for a hero moisturizer when on sale; usual impulse buys capped ~$10–$35 for serums or cleansers.
Dislikes 'all-natural' marketing when it omits allergen lists; requires preservatives listed explicitly.
Patch-tests all new actives on the inner forearm for 72 hours before integrating.
Keeps a small 'quarantine' drawer for new samples for at least two weeks.
Favor water-based sunscreens for makeup layering unless a mineral formula is specifically formulated for sensitive skin.
Avoids heavy occlusives during the day unless skin is particularly dry or in cold months.
Likes brands that provide dermatologist-vetted Q&As on their product pages.
Chooses lightweight, fragrance-free cleansing balms or micellar waters on days with makeup and gentle non-foaming gel cleansers otherwise.
Likes travel-friendly packaging and clear expiry (PAO) symbols.
Avoids 'influencer collabs' unless formulation data and full ingredient lists are provided without paywall.
Prefers ingredient-first recommendations from pharmacists and dermatologists over celebrity endorsements.
Dislikes complicated multi-step regimens; prefers 3–4 core products (cleanser, serum, moisturizer, sunscreen).
Interested in affordable clinical brands sold in drugstores and reputable DTC brands with transparent formulation notes.
shopping_preferences: Buys primarily from direct-to-consumer brand sites during sales and from specialty beauty e-commerce when they have thorough ingredient filters.
Uses influencer codes for small discounts but validates claims through reviews and dermatologist posts.
Prefers free returns and sample programs when available; avoids non-refundable big purchases.
Looks for explicit allergy/allergen disclaimers and preservative lists before committing.
Uses price-tracking browser extensions and waits for holiday sales to buy hero items.
Keeps a wishlist and only purchases hero items when either a sale or a confirmed positive community consensus appears.
Reads at least 10 long-form user reviews (including negative ones) before buying a new active product.
Prefer shops that offer subscription options and trial sizes to reduce risk.
Likes local dermatologists and compounding pharmacies for custom needs and trusts their recommendations highly.
Often buys drugstore 'dupes' for basic items—prefers to reserve budget for a single high-quality moisturizer.
Avoids in-store impulse buys triggered by packaging alone; brings a small list when browsing specialty stores.
Prefers online retailers with robust ingredient search filters.
Checks shipping times and restocks cadence before subscribing to recurring orders.
Follows brand storytelling about labor practices and manufacturing locations.
Uses price boundaries: typically $10–$70 per product, with most purchases under $35.
Keeps receipts and expiry dates in a note app to monitor shelf life and usage rate.
Prefers email newsletters with honest sample offers rather than flashy ad bombardments.
Responds positively to educational content (how a product targets barrier repair), not emotionalized marketing.
Uses community discount codes and prefers to support smaller indie brands when their ethics are clear.
Avoids preorders unless formulation and allergen details are provided upfront.
media_consumption: YouTube channels: dermatologist Q&As, ingredient deep-dives, and product slow-reviews (prefers 15–40 minute videos).
Podcasts: health policy, responsible consumerism, and small-business owner stories.
Reads: long-form investigative journalism about beauty supply chains, dermatology reports, and consumer reviews on sites like The Derm Review.
Forums: active on a few subreddit threads about sensitive skin, frequents community review threads and contributes patch-test notes.
Instagram: follows evidence-based dermatologists and a few minimalist brand accounts; primarily uses it as a visual ingredient board.
TikTok: watches short explainer videos but rarely trusts viral product claims without cross-checking.
Newsletters: subscribes to one or two brand newsletters that offer sample programs.
Books: enjoys accessible science books about the skin and public health primers.
Video consumption habit: watches long-form content in the evenings or on commutes via public transit.
Prefers textual reviews and avoids formats that prioritize aesthetics over ingredient clarity.
Values creator transparency and the disclosure of paid partnerships.
Trusts community moderators and verified medical accounts over unverified influencers.
Uses closed Facebook groups for curated, moderated discussion about patch test experiences.
Saves favorite videos/articles to a reading list for later reference when evaluating a product.
Compares YouTube demonstrations of product texture and spreadability before a purchase.
Enjoys watching compounding pharmacists demonstrate topical formulation at community events or shorts.
Avoids sensational health-claim videos promising 'miracle' fixes for all skin types.
Relies on dermatologist interviews to clarify misunderstanding about actives and side effects.
Likes long comment sections where users describe realistic pros and cons over months of use.
Occasionally creates concise product comparison notes for others in their community.
lifestyle_preferences: Prefers living within walking distance of work and a small grocery store; uses public transit or rideshares occasionally.
Enjoys low-key social gatherings: tea with two friends, museum afternoons, or board game nights.
Cook-at-home most nights using simple, cross-cultural recipes that reflect their mixed heritage.
Maintains a modest capsule wardrobe with a focus on comfort and sustainability.
Exercising 2–3 times per week: brisk walking, yoga, and the occasional paddleboard in summer.
Values sleep hygiene—keeps consistent bedtimes to help skin and mood regulation.
Minimal alcohol consumption and no tobacco use.
Likes to volunteer occasionally at a community clinic or local shelter.
Prioritizes quiet weekends for errands, small social visits, and rest.
Keeps houseplants (pothos, snake plant) to improve indoor air quality and mood.

beliefs: Cruelty-free testing is essential; cosmetic testing on animals is unethical.
Transparency matters: brands should publish full ingredient lists including preservative systems.
Ethical labor practices are as important as environmental claims.
Fragrance in skincare is often unnecessary and frequently triggers allergic reactions.
Ingredient-first education reduces harmful product misuse and unrealistic expectations.
Dermatologists and pharmacists are more trustworthy sources for sensitive-skin guidance than influencers alone.
'Clean' is a marketing term unless fully defined; consumers deserve clear criteria.
Patch testing should be standard practice before adding any new active to a routine.
Minimalist routines are often better for barrier repair than complex, daily experimentation.
Subscription models can be sustainable for budgeting but must offer flexibility and easy cancellation.
Affordable drugstore options can be as efficacious as high-end products for basic hydration and barrier repair.
Brand storytelling without data is insufficient—supporting evidence or clinical testing builds trust.
Representation matters: marketing should reflect mixed-race and non-binary identities authentically.
Small, indie brands deserve scrutiny for ethics and transparency, not automatic praise.
Sustainability is important but should not be used as an excuse for poor labor practices.
Ingredient lists with fewer, well-chosen components can be superior for sensitive skin.
Allergic individuals must advocate for themselves and learn to read labels carefully.
Preservatives are necessary for safety; demonizing all preservatives is counterproductive.
Honest negative reviews are valuable to make informed purchase decisions.
Community forums are a good resource but can amplify confirmation bias; cross-check with medical sources.
Marketing-driven 'miracle' claims are almost always misleading or overblown.
Clinically measured outcomes matter more than anecdotal before/after selfies.
Dermatology should be more accessible and less gatekept for people with budget constraints.
Brands should offer travel and sample sizes to reduce financial risk for sensitive consumers.
Loyalty is earned by consistent, transparent communication and product reliability rather than influencer buzz.
Price is not always an indicator of quality; formulation and concentration matter more.
Ingredient interactions are real—consumers need simple, accessible guides on layering actives safely.
Public health and personal skincare intersect; stress, sleep, diet, and environmental factors influence the skin.
Representation in research matters—clinical trials should include diverse skin types and cultural backgrounds.
Consumers should be wary of over-formalized 'natural' claims that obscure allergenic botanical extracts.
Testing single variables (e.g., one active at a time) yields the most reliable personal data.
Community-driven review standards (like listing skin type, routine, patch test results) increase review utility.
Brands that support third-party audits for cruelty-free and labor standards are more credible.
The beauty industry needs clearer rules around 'clinical' language and what constitutes a tested claim.
Affordability programs and sampled bundles can reduce barriers to care for people with limited budgets.
skills: Competent in pharmacy dispensing workflows, prescription verification, and OTC counseling.
Skilled at reading and parsing INCI ingredient lists and identifying potential allergens or irritants.
Experienced in patch-testing procedures and documenting reactions over a 72-hour period.
Comfortable with basic point-of-sale systems and pharmacy inventory management software.
Able to explain barrier repair concepts (ceramides, cholesterol, fatty acids) in accessible language.
Good at budget management, tracking subscriptions and using spreadsheets to monitor expenses.
Able to research primary literature and trustworthy secondary sources to validate claims.
Basic photo documentation skills: consistent flat-lay imagery and close-ups of textures/labels for comparison.
Confident in applying simple first aid and advising on over-the-counter hydrocortisone usage as appropriate.
Moderate networking skills within local healthcare community—can reach out to dermatology offices to clarify prescriptions.
Time-management: balancing part-time shifts, gig work, and personal commitments.
Customer service skills: calm, empathetic, and patient-centered communication.
Basic social media literacy focused on community moderation and information curation rather than personal branding.
Comfortable with e-commerce functions: subscription management, returns, and sample program navigation.
Can create concise, evidence-based product comparison notes for others.

general: Always reads the full ingredient list before trying a product and avoids products that list vague 'fragrance'.
Keeps a small notebook and an app-based tracker for patch test results, usage frequency, and perceived irritation.
Typically tests only one new active per month to isolate reactions.
Often buys travel/sample sizes first and stores them in a dedicated drawer for at least two weeks.
Prefers text or email when scheduling appointments; avoids phone calls unless necessary.
Checks community forums and a dermatologist's page before trying a trending product.
Uses coupon codes and waits for 20–30% discounts for more expensive hero items.
Recycles and repurposes packaging when possible, keeping an inventory of small jars for decanting.
Maintains a pared-down routine (cleanse, hydrate, moisturize, sunscreen) and only adds actives temporarily.
Avoids heavy cosmetic routines before work shifts to reduce potential irritation during long days under fluorescent light.

morning: Wakes around 7:30 AM on workdays to allow time for a calm routine.
Quick rinse or gentle gel cleanse if skin feels oily from night.
Applies a lightweight hydrating serum (hyaluronic acid) and a ceramide-rich moisturizer.
Uses a non-chemical mineral or sensitive-skin sunscreen when going outside.
Prepares a simple breakfast (oatmeal or toast) and checks messages and a few saved YouTube videos while eating.
Glances through community threads for any urgent product recall or allergy alerts.
workday: Arrives at pharmacy 15 minutes early to prepare OTC area and check inventory.
Takes extra care to keep hands moisturized during shifts to avoid barrier breakdown.
Suggests fragrance-free OTC products to customers with sensitivities and makes notes for follow-ups.
Uses brief breaks to scan product pages for upcoming sales or restocks.
Logs any unusual customer-reported reactions and communicates with supervising pharmacist as needed.
evening: Cleanses with a gentle, non-foaming cleanser after work.
Applies targeted treatments only if no signs of irritation during the day.
Does a 72-hour diary on any recent patch tests before introducing a new product.
Watches a long-form dermatologist Q&A or reads a well-sourced article before bed.
Spends 20–30 minutes journaling finances, skin notes, and a to-do list for the next day.
weekend: Visits a thrift store or small neighborhood market.
Schedules a longer skin-check with a dermatologist or practices deeper research on a product they're considering.
Meets one or two friends for coffee or a quiet museum visit.
Tries one sample product on a designated patch test area and logs the outcome.
Cooks a larger batch of food for the week to save money and reduce decision fatigue.

physical: Has clinically diagnosed contact allergies to certain fragrances, some botanical extracts (e.g., certain essential oils), and specific preservatives (documented in an allergy patch test panel).
Skin type: sensitive, with a history of barrier impairment, episodic dermatitis flares (eczema-like), and reactive redness/stinging to strong actives.
Regularly uses ceramide-containing moisturizers and occlusive petrolatum at night when flares occur.
Avoids frequent chemical exfoliation; uses lactic acid or mandelic acid at low frequency when recommended by a dermatologist.
Sees a dermatologist every 6–12 months and a local allergist for patch test updates when necessary.
No chronic systemic illnesses disclosed; occasional seasonal allergies and mild exercise-induced asthma managed with an inhaler as needed.
Non-smoker, low alcohol intake, moderate caffeine consumption (1–2 cups/day).
Vaccinations up to date according to standard adult schedules.
Maintains a simple supplement routine: vitamin D in winter and a daily multivitamin when finances allow.
Has occasional sleep disruption tied to work schedule changes and anxiety around finances; practices sleep hygiene to mitigate.
mental: Mild generalized anxiety, particularly related to finances and health; manageable with routines and occasional CBT-based strategies.
Practical coping strategies: journaling, breathing exercises, and structured to-do lists.
Prefers low-cost or community mental health resources when needed; has used a sliding-scale counselor once.
Social anxiety in large crowds; prefers small-group interactions and planned social obligations.
Motivated and resilient, but experiences low-grade stress during prolonged financial unpredictability.
health.medical_history_summary: No major hospitalizations. Allergies are documented via patch testing. Regular dermatology follow-ups for barrier management. No major medications aside from occasional OTC hydrocortisone and symptomatic inhaler use for asthma.

entries.name: Maya Bennett
entries.description: Works as an elementary school paraprofessional. Supportive, pragmatic, checks in weekly, provides occasional financial help or hand-me-downs. Encourages stable work and values education.

entries.name: Chris Bennett
entries.description: Freelance mechanic. Practical advice-giver, sometimes skeptical of online trends, but respects Ari's careful approach. Lives two hours away; visits for holidays.

entries.name: Zoe Bennett
entries.description: Close in playful, low-key ways; Ari mentors Zoe on budgeting and basic skincare for teen acne; enjoys shared cooking experiments.

entries.name: Jordan
entries.description: Non-binary roommate in their twenties; shares thrifted clothing tips, babysits each other's plants, and co-manages grocery runs.

entries.name: Priya
entries.description: Works at a nearby dermatology clinic; provides occasional clinical context and helps Ari interpret dermatology statements.

entries.name: Evan
entries.description: Regularly exchanges product notes and runs co-owned spreadsheet trackers of sale cycles and subscription overlaps.

entries.name: Dr. Alvarez
entries.description: Sees Ari for scheduled check-ins and prescription clarification; collaborative and practical in recommendations.

entries.name: Sam
entries.description: Direct manager; values Ari's careful counseling and sometimes delegates OTC advisory tasks to them during busy shifts.
Not currently in a committed relationship; open to dating but cautious about emotional and financial investment. Prefers slow-developing relationships built on mutual respect and clear communication.

other_facts: Grew up in a Midwestern suburb with a mixed-race household—one parent with East Asian heritage and one with European ancestry—leading to a blended culinary and cultural upbringing.
Speaks conversational Spanish from high-school classes and family interactions; picks up basic phrases when traveling.
First job was at a grocery store produce section at 16; learned inventory rotation and the value of steady customer interactions.
Has a small collection of labelled travel-size jars with decanted product samples dating back three years.
Was once chemically burned by an over-the-counter peel in late teens; that experience led to an ongoing cautiousness and a focus on barrier repair.
Keeps a folder of old product labels and notes that reads like a personal 'formulation notebook' with observations about textures, absorption times, and irritation incidents.
Has a 6-month rule: if a product causes delayed irritation after 2–3 weeks, they will write a detailed post to warn the community.
Attends an annual skincare meetup hosted by a local dermatology practice when schedules allow.
Has a ritual of 'no-phones' for the first 30 minutes after waking to reduce decision fatigue and allow the skin routine to be calm.
Once tried a viral product and had to return three items after a negative reaction; that experience cemented conservative purchasing behaviors.
Volunteers occasionally at a community health clinic helping with basic intake and handing out sample-size skincare donated by local brands.
Has a small emergency kit with fragrance-free hydrocortisone, emollient ointment, and hypoallergenic sunscreen.
Is the unofficial 'label reader' among friend groups; friends ask Ari to check ingredient lists on gifting occasions.
Tracks product usage rate meticulously to avoid waste and to time subscriptions with sales.
Has a favorite cheap caffeine option: a local ethnic bakery’s cardamom coffee that pairs with reading research abstracts.
Was once featured in a community magazine for young workers doing thoughtful budget prioritization.
Prefers to spend limited extra money on experiences (museum memberships) rather than material goods.
Learnt basic small-claims budgeting from a family member after a prior roommate left unpaid bills; it informs Ari's conservative approach to renting and shared expenses.
Owns one small terrarium that they keep meticulously watered; plant care is soothing and helps regulate anxiety.
Has a vivid memory of their first dermatology appointment when a dermatologist explained ceramides; it changed their approach and career interest toward pharmacy tech work.
Keeps a list of local dermatologists, compounding pharmacies, and trustworthy DTC brands in a note app for quick reference.
Enjoys swapping small sample bottles and coordinates small ‘swap nights’ with two close friends to try new things safely.
Has a particular dislike of strongly scented laundry detergents and chooses hypoallergenic detergents for clothes and bedding.
Enjoys participating in community science events and has taken part in a local volunteer skin-health survey.
Remembers being told as a teenager that 'all skin is different' and took that as permission to be patient—now passes that on to others.
Uses a pale blue silicone tester (small adhesive patch) to trial product textures under low-stress conditions.
Has an anxiety-triggered habit of double-checking subscription deliveries two days before they ship to avoid duplicate charges.
Once helped an older customer at the pharmacy navigate sunscreen choices and felt rewarded by the meaningful conversation.
Is motivated to one day create a plain-language guide to preservative types and why they matter for skin safety.
Keeps an offline backup of favorite product lists and ingredient notes in a small Moleskine notebook separated by categories.
Has a soft spot for small, honest brands started by people with dermatology backgrounds.
Prefers relationships—professional or personal—that give space, clear communication, and the ability to say 'I need a quiet night in'.
Has a clear memory of learning to budget for a skincare 'hero' moisturizer and how achieving that purchase felt like a milestone.
Enjoys quiet nighttime walks in their neighborhood as a way to decompress and reflect on the day.
Values being a practical resource for friends and online community members, and sometimes receives small gifts of gratitude (tea, soap) from helped individuals.
Is cautious about joining too many subscription services at once and staggers them to avoid overlapping products and wasted money.
Finds comfort in rituals: same order of application in skincare, same playlist when cooking, same route to work.
Has a preference for matte, low-shine packaging because it feels less attention-grabbing and more private.
Maintains a list of 'trustworthy' and 'to-avoid' brands based on personal reactions, clinical evidence, and labor-ethics research.
Dreams occasionally about one day running a tiny online shop offering curated, allergy-conscious sample kits for others with sensitive skin.
